summary: Jeff Atwood critiques the argument that J2EE's complexity is a feature rather than a bug. Responding to a study showing VS.NET was twice as productive as J2EE at one-tenth the cost, he addresses pushback from Java developers who claimed that difficult tools 'weed out weak developers' and produce better code. Atwood argues this reasoning is backwards—bad programmers write bad code regardless of tools, while good programmers benefit from productivity gains without sacrificing quality. He dismisses the notion that tool difficulty correlates with code quality, calling it an attempt to reframe a weakness as a strength.
key_points: A commissioned study found VS.NET twice as productive as J2EE at roughly one-tenth the cost
Dismissing a study solely because it was sponsored (rather than engaging with its substance) is intellectually lazy
The argument that 'difficult tools produce better code' inverts reality—tool complexity doesn't improve developer quality
RAD tools make good programmers more productive without causing them to write worse code
Bad programmers write bad code regardless of tooling; good programmers maintain quality while benefiting from easier tools
money_quotes: VS.NET is about twice as productive as either of the two IBM J2EE environments, at approximately one-tenth the cost.
Disregarding the report sight unseen because Microsoft sponsored it is like disregarding someone's opinion based on their ethnicity.
J2EE weeds out the weak developers! It rampages through the land, leaving a wake of crushed and broken developers weeping in its path!
At any point in any of those projects, can you ever remember saying to yourself: Developing applications is far too easy!
Bad programmers write bad code. Good programmers write good code. RAD lets bad programmers write bad code faster. RAD does NOT cause good programmers to suddenly start writing bad code.
What I find amusing is that someone would actually try to invert this argument, proposing that bad tools are good because they force you to produce better code.
key_insight: Tool difficulty doesn't filter for developer quality—it just makes everyone less productive, while bad programmers remain bad regardless of their toolchain.
